1: The word of YHWH comes to Ezekiel with the command to make known to Jerusalem her abominations. The extended allegory of chapter 16 is the fullest expression of the husband-wife covenant metaphor in scripture. Its Christological significance lies entirely at the end (Ezekiel 16:59-63): the everlasting covenant YHWH will establish despite all infidelity anticipates the new covenant established in Christ's blood. Every verse of the allegory narrates the condition that makes the new covenant necessary and the grace that makes it possible.
2: The direct divine address — "Son of man, make Jerusalem know her abominations" — has its NT echo in Christ's letters to the seven churches in Revelation 2-3, each of which begins with "I know your works." The prophetic role of making a beloved community know its failures is one that Christ inherits and fulfills — not to condemn but to call to repentance (Revelation 3:19: "those whom I love I reprove and discipline").
3: The Amorite/Hittite origin of Jerusalem: her father was an Amorite and her mother a Hittite. The pagan origins of the city before David's conquest underline that YHWH's choice of Jerusalem was entirely grace, not merit. Christologically, this parallels Paul's point about the inclusion of gentiles: "you were alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise" (Ephesians 2:12) — yet Christ makes peace. The gracious choosing of the spiritually orphaned is the gospel logic embedded in the allegory's opening.
4: The birth scene: not washed, not salted, not swaddled, not cared for. The complete abandonment of the newborn child by its birth family represents the total absence of any prior merit or preparation. Christologically, this is the condition of those whom Christ finds and saves — not those who are already prepared and worthy, but those utterly without covenantal standing. The parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:11-32) echoes the foundling logic: the son ends up among swine, completely abandoned, before the father runs to meet him.
5: "No eye pitied you, to do any of these things to you out of compassion for you; but you were cast out on the open field, for you were abhorred, on the day that you were born." The total absence of pity anticipates the divine pity that replaces it. Christologically, this is the condition of the world before the incarnation: "while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8). The pity that no one had, Christ supplies completely — the one who was himself "despised and rejected" (Isaiah 53:3) became the source of all compassion.
6: "Live!" — the double command: YHWH passes by the abandoned child lying in its blood and says twice: "Live! Live!" (beDamayich chayi). This is the founding divine word over the people — not condemnation but creation. Christologically, this double "live!" anticipates the double resurrection-life that Christ brings: "I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25); "he who believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live" (John 11:25). The life spoken over the blood-stained foundling is grace without condition — the same pattern as justification by faith.
7: YHWH causes the child to multiply "like a plant of the field" and grow up into full adulthood. The growth metaphor recalls the "plant" and "branch" imagery applied to the Messiah (Isaiah 53:2: "he grew up before him like a young plant"; Jeremiah 23:5: "righteous Branch"). The full physical development of the woman in Ezekiel 16:7 parallels the full humanity of the incarnate Christ — not a partial or docetic humanity but the complete assumption of human nature.
8: The spreading of the garment: the marriage covenant: "I spread the corner of my garment over you and covered your nakedness; I made my vow to you and entered into a covenant with you, declares the Lord YHWH." The kanaf (corner of garment) spread over the naked woman is the marriage gesture of Boaz for Ruth (Ruth 3:9) and the archetype of all covenantal covering. Christologically, this is fulfilled in Christ who "loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle" (Ephesians 5:25-27).
9: The washing with water, the anointing with oil, the dressing in embroidered clothing — the transformation from abandoned foundling to bride is accomplished by YHWH's action, not the foundling's merit. Christologically, this pattern recurs in Christian baptism: the washing with water (John 3:5; Ephesians 5:26; Titus 3:5), the anointing with the Spirit (the chrism of 1 John 2:20, 27), and the clothing in righteousness (Galatians 3:27: "clothed with Christ"; Revelation 3:18: white garments). The bridal preparation accomplished by YHWH maps directly to the preparation of the church by Christ.
10: The fine linen, silk, and embroidered work — the rich clothing given by YHWH. The bride in Revelation 19:8 is "clothed with fine linen, bright and pure" — the fine linen representing the righteous deeds of the saints but as YHWH's gift (Revelation 19:8: "it was granted her to clothe herself with fine linen"). The gifts listed in Ezekiel 16:10-13 all reappear in the New Jerusalem imagery of Revelation 21-22, where the adornment of the bride-city with precious materials represents YHWH's ultimate gift.
11: Bracelets, necklace, ring, crown — the full adornment of royalty and marriage. The jewelry catalogue anticipates the removal of all these items in judgment (Ezekiel 16:39) and their eschatological restoration in the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2: the holy city "coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband"). Christ is the one who makes the final bridal adornment possible by first bearing the nakedness of judgment (Ezekiel 16:39 reversed).
12: The nose ring and ear ornaments completing the bridal adornment. In Genesis 24:22, 30, the servant of Abraham adorns Rebekah with a nose ring and bracelets as the seal of the marriage covenant — the same gesture here. The Christological typology: as Abraham's servant sought a bride for Isaac, the Holy Spirit draws the church as the bride for Christ (John 3:29: the friend of the bridegroom rejoices at the bridegroom's voice).
13: The beauty achieved through YHWH's gifts: fine flour, honey, and oil — royal food. The beauty is entirely the product of covenantal grace. The Christological application is Paul's: "I am what I am by the grace of God" (1 Corinthians 15:10). The beauty of the church before Christ is not inherent but bestowed — "by grace you have been saved through faith... it is the gift of God" (Ephesians 2:8).
14: The fame of the beauty spreading among the nations: YHWH's own beauty perfected on Jerusalem. The Christological fulfillment: the beauty of Christ shown through the church is YHWH's own beauty given to the world. Isaiah 60:1-3 sees the nations coming to the light of Jerusalem's restored glory — a glory that is not Jerusalem's own but YHWH's given to her. Revelation 21:24: "by its light will the nations walk, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it."
15: The bride trusts in her beauty and uses it for prostitution. The trust in beauty rather than in the Giver of beauty is the root sin. Christologically, this maps to the idolatry of self-sufficiency — the church (like Israel) can be tempted to trust its own spiritual accomplishments or institutional beauty rather than Christ. The antidote in Revelation 3:17-18 is identical in logic: "you do not realize that you are wretched, pitiful, poor, blind and naked. I counsel you to buy from me gold refined in the fire... and white clothes to wear... and salve to put on your eyes."
16: Garments made into high-place shrines, spread out for the idols. The gifts of the marriage covenant are re-purposed for idolatrous use. This apostasy is the background against which Hebrews 10:29 warns: "how much worse punishment... will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God, and has profaned the blood of the covenant by which he was sanctified." The wedding gifts treated as prostitute's tools anticipate the NT warning about the greater accountability of those who have received covenantal gifts and misused them.
17: The silver and gold of YHWH's gifts made into images of men and worshipped. The created replaces the Creator — the precise definition of idolatry in Romans 1:23-25. Christologically, the reversal of this idolatry is the incarnation: the Creator himself becomes man (eikon), so that human beings can see God in a human face without making forbidden images. In Christ, the prohibition on making images of God (Exodus 20:4) is fulfilled rather than violated — he is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15).
18: The embroidered garments covering the idols; the oil and incense set before them — YHWH's gifts redirected to rivals. The jealousy of YHWH for his gifts (Exodus 20:5: "I am a jealous God") is the other side of his covenant love. Christologically, this jealousy is the motivating force behind the cross: the love that cannot bear to see the beloved destroyed is also the love that would rather die than lose the beloved. "God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but in order that the world might be saved through him" (John 3:17).
19: The fine flour, oil, and honey — the very food of Ezekiel 16:13 — offered to the idols. YHWH's daily provision redirected to false gods. The bread of life imagery in John 6 (Jesus as the bread from heaven) is relevant here: the manna/bread that sustains life is either received from YHWH or redirected to idols. Jesus names himself "the living bread that came down from heaven" (John 6:51) — the true food that was always YHWH's gift, now given in its fullness in the incarnation.
20: Child sacrifice: sons and daughters: The sons and daughters born to YHWH sacrificed to idols — was the prostitution insufficient? The sacrifice of YHWH's covenant children to Molech is the nadir of the chapter. Christologically, this horror is met by the inverse sacrifice: God gives his own Son (not a stolen son, but the eternal Son) — "he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all" (Romans 8:32). The children sacrificed to Molech are replaced by the Child sacrificed by the Father for the salvation of all the children who should have died.
21: Slaughtering YHWH's children and offering them as food to the idols — the worst phrase of the oracle. Christologically, this is countered by the Lord's Supper: where pagan child sacrifice offered human flesh to idols, the Eucharist receives the one true sacrifice of Christ as bread and cup — the body and blood freely given by God himself for human life (John 6:53-55). The eucharistic inversion of child sacrifice is radical: instead of children dying to feed idols, God's Son dies to feed the covenant children.
22: In all the abominations, Jerusalem did not remember the days of her youth — lying in blood, naked and exposed. The foundling origin (Ezekiel 16:4-6) is forgotten in the prosperity of adulthood. The NT parallel is the warning of Revelation 2:4-5: "you have abandoned the love you had at first. Remember therefore from where you have fallen; repent, and do the works you did at first." The call to remember the foundling origin is a Christological call to remember that one's entire existence is grace.
23: Woe pronounced upon Jerusalem after all her wickedness — the double woe marking the oracle's intensification. The woe formula in Luke 11:42-52 (Jesus's woes on the Pharisees) follows the same prophetic tradition: the covenant community confronted with its accumulated failures is met with woe that is simultaneously judgment and lament. Christ weeps over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) — the woe that judges is also the woe that grieves.
24: Building a vaulted chamber (gev) and a high place in every square — the infrastructure of prostitution made permanent. The Christological inversion: Jesus's body is the true temple (John 2:19-22), and those who come to him enter a house of prayer for all nations (Mark 11:17), not a house of prostitution. The sacred geography that Israel corrupted Christ restores — his body is the new sacred space.
25: High places at every street corner and prostitution offered to every passerby. The indiscriminate availability of the apostasy — no corner of Jerusalem without its site of unfaithfulness. Christologically, this total corruption of the sacred everywhere creates the condition that requires a total redemption everywhere. Paul's "where sin increased, grace abounded all the more" (Romans 5:20) is the theological response to this universal pollution.
26: Prostitution with the Egyptians — neighbors with large members (gedoley basar) — provoking YHWH's anger. The geopolitical alliance with Egypt as an act of covenant infidelity. Christologically, the impulse to seek security in worldly power rather than in YHWH is the same temptation Jesus rejected in the wilderness (Matthew 4:8-10): "all these kingdoms I will give you if you will fall down and worship me." The refusal of earthly power-seeking is Christ's counter to Israel's Egypt-lust.
27: YHWH stretches out his hand and reduces Jerusalem's portion, handing her to the will of those who hate her. The progressive divine discipline — reducing provision before final judgment — reflects the covenant-curse escalation of Leviticus 26:14-39. Christologically, God's disciplinary love is expressed in Hebrews 12:6-11: "the Lord disciplines the one he loves." The reduction of provision is not rejection but corrective grace.
28: Prostitution with Assyria next — insatiable; still not satisfied. The insatiability of covenant unfaithfulness — each alliance leads to more, not less, need. Augustine's famous formulation (Confessions I.1) resonates: "our heart is restless, until it repose in Thee." The insatiability of idolatry is the negative image of the infinite satisfaction offered in Christ: "whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again" (John 4:14).
29: Prostitution extended even to Chaldea — the land of merchants — and still not satisfied. The exhaustive reach of apostasy: every available imperial alliance was pursued. Christologically, the utter exhaustion of human-effort satisfaction-seeking is the precondition for recognizing that Christ is "the bread of life" — the one food that ends hunger rather than increasing it (John 6:35).
30: How sick is your heart — doing all these things as an audacious/brazen prostitute! The divine lament over the state of Jerusalem's heart. Heart-sickness in the prophets (Jeremiah 17:9: "the heart is deceitful above all things and desperately sick") is the diagnosis that requires Christ as the great physician (Mark 2:17: "those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick"). The divine lament is not abandonment but the expression of a love that grieves over what it sees.
31: Building a vaulted chamber at every street head and a high place in every square — yet unlike a prostitute you did not receive payment. The paradox: Israel's unfaithfulness was not even financially profitable; she paid her lovers rather than receiving payment. This inverts normal transactional logic. Christologically, this economic inversion points to the gospel: it is YHWH who pays the ultimate price (the Son) for the unfaithful bride, not the bride who purchases anything. Grace is the complete inversion of merit-economy.
32: The adulterous wife who takes strangers instead of her husband. The marriage covenant as the controlling metaphor — this is not just commercial unfaithfulness but personal betrayal. The NT picks up this covenant-marriage metaphor when Paul says the church is presented to Christ as a pure virgin (2 Corinthians 11:2-3), and warns against the "serpent deceived Eve by his cunning" — the same covenant infidelity pattern.
33: Gifts are given to all lovers, but Jerusalem gives her gifts to all her lovers and bribes them to come to her. The economy of grace distorted: where YHWH gives freely, Jerusalem pays. The distortion of grace-economy into bribery is the background for Jesus's confrontation with the money-changers in the temple (John 2:14-16) — sacred space had been turned into a market, precisely the transformation this chapter depicts at the spiritual level.
34: The reversal: Jerusalem is opposite of all other prostitutes — she gives payment and receives none. The complete economic inversion of covenant unfaithfulness. Paul uses economic metaphors for the gospel in the opposite direction: "you were bought with a price" (1 Corinthians 6:20; Ezekiel 7:23) — Christ paid the ultimate price to purchase the church as his own. Where Jerusalem was paying to be unfaithful, believers are bought by Christ to belong to him.
35: Therefore, O prostitute, hear the word of YHWH — the transition from indictment to sentence. The "therefore" marks the logical connection between the documented infidelity and the coming judgment. Christologically, the transition from judgment to promise that begins at Ezekiel 16:59-63 is only possible because someone absorbed the "therefore" — the one who "was made sin for us" (2 Corinthians 5:21) so that the verdict could be reversed.
36: Because your bronze (shame/nakedness) was poured out and your nakedness uncovered in your prostitutions... the accumulated guilt brought to a reckoning. The judicial language — guilt enumerated and charged to the account — is the precise situation Paul describes: "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23), for which the answer is "justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus" (Romans 3:24).
37: YHWH will gather all the lovers who pleased Jerusalem and all she hated; he will gather them against her from all around and uncover her nakedness before them. The judgment uses the very relationships that were instruments of apostasy. Christologically, this is the mechanism of the cross in reverse — where the nations gathered against Jerusalem in judgment, Christ gathered the nations to himself by being lifted up (John 12:32: "when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself").
38: Judged as adulteresses and those who shed blood are judged — blood guilt and sexual infidelity as the twin charges. Both charges are addressed by Christ: the blood guilt is cleansed by "the blood of Jesus his Son" that "cleanses us from all sin" (1 John 1:7); the covenant infidelity is restored by the new covenant "in my blood" (Luke 22:20).
39: Handed over to the lovers who will strip off finery and beautiful jewels, leaving naked and bare — the complete reversal of Ezekiel 16:9-14. The stripping judgment is the inverse of the bridal adornment. Christologically, Christ was "stripped of his clothes" (Matthew 27:28, 35) — bearing the judgment-stripping so that the church might be re-robed in the righteousness of God (2 Corinthians 5:21; Isaiah 61:10: "he has clothed me with the garments of salvation").
40: The assembly against Jerusalem, stoned with stones, cut with swords. The community execution — judicial killing of the covenant-breaking city. The collective execution by stoning anticipates Christ's own execution outside the city (Hebrews 13:12: "Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood"). He was executed as the guilty city ought to have been.
41: Houses burned, judgments executed before the eyes of many women — the public and visible punishment. The publicity of the judgment corresponds to the publicity of Christ's atonement: "God put [Christ] forward as a propitiation... to show his righteousness at the present time" (Romans 3:25-26). The public nature of both judgment and redemption is integral to the covenant's integrity before the watching nations.
42: YHWH's fury will be satisfied; his jealousy will depart from Jerusalem, and he will be quiet and no longer angry. The exhaustion of divine wrath — not its eternal continuance. Christologically, Isaiah 53:11 says: "out of the anguish of his soul he shall see and be satisfied." The satisfaction of divine wrath is not denied but displaced onto the servant. The fury is exhausted — either in judgment on Jerusalem or in the substitutionary death of Christ.
43: Because Jerusalem did not remember the days of her youth but has enraged YHWH with all these things, he will bring her conduct back upon her own head. The judicial principle of reaping what was sown (Galatians 6:7) is here applied to Jerusalem. The Christological alternative is substitution: Christ "redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Galatians 3:13) — the conduct returned upon the head of the servant rather than upon the people.
44: Jerusalem shall not commit lewdness in addition to all her other abominations. The oracle turns to the comparison with sisters. The comparison structure — worse than Samaria and Sodom — is a Christological preparation: it shows that the entire covenant community failed, requiring a Savior not just for the outer failures of pagan nations but for the inner failures of the elect people. "Judgment must begin at the household of God" (1 Peter 4:17).
45: The proverb about the mother being like the daughter — the family-resemblance of sin. Jerusalem's Amorite/Hittite mixed origin (Ezekiel 16:3) was always in her. Christologically, this is the logic of original sin that requires new birth: "that which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit" (John 3:6). The new birth that Christ offers (John 3:3-7) creates a new family resemblance — to the Father rather than to the Amorite mother.
46: The sister Samaria who lived to Jerusalem's north with her daughters. Samaria represents the northern kingdom, destroyed by Assyria in 722 BCE. Bringing Samaria into the comparison raises the stakes: even the already-judged sister is less guilty. Christologically, the inclusion of Samaria in the comparison foreshadows Jesus's ministry in Samaria (John 4) — the "worse" sister is also the object of the Messiah's saving attention, fulfilling the promise of the everlasting covenant (Ezekiel 16:60-63).
47: Jerusalem behaved so wickedly that her sisters appeared righteous by comparison. The rhetorical shock is the point: Israel's sin exceeded even that of the pagans. Paul makes the same move in Romans 2:17-24 to show that Jewish covenant privilege did not produce Jewish covenant faithfulness — the one with greater light sinned against greater light. This prepares the universal scope of Christ's atonement: "all have sinned" (Romans 3:23) without exception.
48: As YHWH lives, Sodom and Samaria have not done what Jerusalem did. The oath-form of the divine declaration — swearing by his own life — emphasizes the certainty and severity of the verdict. Christologically, YHWH's oath-language (Hebrews 6:13-18 emphasizes that God swore by himself since there was no greater) points to the absolute trustworthiness of covenant promises. The oath of judgment in Ezekiel 16:48 is met by the oath of restoration in Ezekiel 16:60-63.
49: Sodom's sin: pride, fullness of food, prosperous ease; she did not aid the poor and needy. The famous Sodom-passage that identifies Sodom's sin as economic self-sufficiency and neglect of the poor rather than merely sexual sin. Christologically, this diagnosis is the background for Jesus's teaching on wealth and the poor (Luke 16:19-31: the rich man and Lazarus, with its explicit Sodom-like judgment), and for James 2:15-16: failing to provide for the destitute is the equivalent of faith without works.
50: Sodom and daughters were haughty and committed abomination before YHWH; YHWH removed them when he saw it. The comprehensive Sodomite failure — pride, neglect, sexual sin — is all encompassed in the divine sight. Christologically, "God sees in secret" (Matthew 6:4, 6, 18) is both warning and comfort: the God who saw Sodom's sins also sees acts of love done in secret and rewards them.
51: Samaria has not committed half your sins; Jerusalem has multiplied abominations more than her sisters. The final comparison: Jerusalem worse than Samaria, who was worse than Sodom. The escalation makes Jerusalem the paradigm of covenant failure. This is the same logic Paul uses in Romans 7 to show that even the best expression of covenant obedience (Torah-keeping) cannot produce true righteousness — a Savior is needed from outside the covenant system as well as within it.
52: Jerusalem must bear her disgrace — she has made her sisters appear righteous. The shame of the "superior" covenant people appearing worse than those outside the covenant. This shame is what Paul says Christ took on: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Galatians 3:13). The shame that Jerusalem must bear Christ took upon himself (Hebrews 12:2: "enduring the cross, despising the shame").
53: YHWH will restore the fortunes of Sodom and her daughters, Samaria and her daughters, and Jerusalem's captives — restoration even for Sodom! This verse is one of the most surprising in Ezekiel: the restoration promise extends even to Sodom. Christologically, this anticipates what John sees in Revelation: "the nations" and "the kings of the earth" bringing their glory into the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24-26) — the most surprising inclusion of the apparently irredeemably lost. The wideness of the restoration mercy is as wide as the wideness of Christ's atoning work.
54: Jerusalem shall bear her disgrace and be ashamed of all that she has done, becoming a consolation to her sisters. The comfort of knowing others have fallen similarly — a negative consolation. Christologically, the community of shame that the exile creates becomes the community of restoration — the same people who were a scandal become a testimony. 2 Corinthians 1:3-5: "God... comforts us in all our affliction, so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction."
55: Sodom and her daughters shall return to their former state; Samaria and her daughters shall return to their former state; and Jerusalem and her daughters shall return to their former state. The triple restoration: all three — Sodom, Samaria, Jerusalem — return to their former state. Christologically, the former state is not the goal; the former state was already corrupt. The new covenant (Ezekiel 16:60-63) goes beyond restoration to transformation. Christ does not merely restore Eden but creates a new creation.
56: Was not Sodom your sister a byword in your mouth in the day of your pride? Jerusalem's former contempt for Sodom is now reversed — she has become worse. The irony of the morally superior looking down on the inferior and then becoming worse is a consistent Gospel theme: "Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log that is in your own eye?" (Matthew 7:3). Christ calls his own people to a radical self-examination rather than comparison with others.
57: Before your wickedness was uncovered, you were like a taunt to the daughters of Syria and her neighbors, and the daughters of Philistia who contemned you from all around. The contempt of the surrounding nations — the same nations who will later gloat over Jerusalem's fall (Ezekiel 25:3, 8, 15). The exposure of wickedness changes the community's standing before the nations. Christologically, the transparent exposure of sin is the precondition for the transparent display of grace: "before your wickedness was uncovered" — the uncovering is the crisis that makes redemption possible.
58: Jerusalem bears the penalty of her lewdness and her abominations, declares YHWH. The judicial closure of the indictment: bearing the penalty. Christologically, "bearing" the penalty is exactly what Christ does: "he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree" (1 Peter 2:24); "surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows" (Isaiah 53:4). The one who should bear the penalty does not, because the Servant of YHWH bears it instead.
59: YHWH will deal with Jerusalem as she has done — breaking the covenant after swearing the oath. The legal principle of covenant-breach consequences is the foundation for what follows. The "yes, but..." of Ezekiel 16:60-63 is the hinge of the whole chapter: even covenant-breach does not end the covenant for YHWH, because it was his covenant to begin with, established by his oath. The new covenant rests on Christ's faithfulness, not ours — "even if we are faithless, he remains faithful" (2 Timothy 2:13).
60: The everlasting covenant — the chapter's Christological climax: "Yet I will remember my covenant with you in the days of your youth, and I will establish with you an everlasting covenant." The divine "yet" (va'ani) — I myself — overriding all the preceding unfaithfulness. The new covenant established here is the one that Jeremiah 31:31-34 describes and Hebrews 8-10 identifies with the blood of Christ. "I will establish an everlasting covenant" is fulfilled in "the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, the great shepherd of the sheep, by the blood of the eternal covenant" (Hebrews 13:20).
61: The sisters received as daughters: Jerusalem will remember her ways and be ashamed; she will receive Samaria and Sodom as daughters — "not on account of the covenant with you." The inclusion of the sisters — even Sodom — in the covenantal restoration "not on account of the covenant" is grace beyond covenant. Christologically, this is Paul's argument in Romans 9-11: the inclusion of gentiles is pure grace, not covenant extension. Christ brings in those who were not his people so that they might become the people of God (1 Peter 2:10; Hosea 2:23).
62: YHWH will establish his covenant with Jerusalem, and she shall know that he is YHWH. The recognition formula that has closed nearly every oracle in the book of Ezekiel (yada'tem ki-ani YHWH) here closes the most personally intimate oracle — the marriage allegory. The knowledge of YHWH that is the goal of all the judgments and restorations is the intimate knowledge of the covenant relationship: John 17:3 — "this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent." Christ mediates the knowing that Israel always failed to maintain.
63: Atonement and silenced shame: "that you may remember and be confounded, and never open your mouth again because of your shame, when I atone for you for all that you have done, declares the Lord YHWH." The final verse of Ezekiel 16 is a complete Gospel summary: YHWH atones (kapparta) for everything that was done. The shame is not denied but silenced by grace. Christologically, this is fulfilled in the one who is both the atoner and the atoned-for: "he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world" (1 John 2:2). The mouth silenced by shame becomes the mouth opened in praise (Psalms 40:3).
